era_context: The late 1720s sit within the consolidation phase of Europe's post-Utrecht order, the mature Qing dynasty under the Yongzheng Emperor's centralizing reforms, the twilight of the Ottoman Tulip Era, and accelerating European colonial extraction in the Americas, West Africa, and South Asia. The Enlightenment was gaining intellectual momentum in France and Britain, while the Maratha Confederacy was ascending as a major South Asian power against the declining Mughal Empire. In the Americas, French and British colonial expansion pushed into indigenous territories, generating both trade dependencies and violent resistance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond the Atlantic slave-trading coast and Ethiopia
Interior Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Laos, upland Burma)
Aboriginal Australia and interior Pacific Islands
Interior South America beyond Iberian colonial towns
Central Asia beyond Qing and Russian frontier zones

events.title: Treaty of Seville ends Anglo-Spanish War
events.description: On 9 November 1729, Great Britain, France, and Spain signed the Treaty of Seville, ending the Anglo-Spanish War of 1727–1729. Spain agreed to restore British commercial privileges in the Spanish Americas, including the asiento (slave-trade monopoly) and annual ship concessions originally granted at Utrecht in 1713. In return, Britain and France guaranteed Spanish succession rights to the Italian duchies of Parma, Piacenza, and Tuscany for the Infante Don Carlos. The treaty represented a significant diplomatic victory for British Prime Minister Robert Walpole, who pursued a policy of peace to protect commercial interests, though it left unresolved tensions over Gibraltar and colonial smuggling that would reignite within a decade.

sources.name: Text of the Treaty of Seville (1729), preserved in British and Spanish diplomatic archives
Jeremy Black, British Foreign Policy in the Age of Walpole (1985)
Jean McLachlan, Trade and Peace with Old Spain, 1667–1750 (1940)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in multiple state archives. Terms and date well established.

events.title: Natchez Revolt against the French at Fort Rosalie
events.description: On 28 November 1729, the Natchez people launched a coordinated surprise attack on the French colonial settlement at Fort Rosalie (present-day Natchez, Mississippi), killing approximately 230 French colonists—nearly the entire male population of the settlement—and taking around 300 women, children, and enslaved Africans captive. The attack was provoked by the French commandant Sieur de Chépart's demand that the Natchez vacate their principal village, including the sacred White Apple site, to make way for a tobacco plantation. The revolt was the most devastating indigenous attack on a French colonial post in Louisiana and triggered a brutal retaliatory campaign in 1730 that effectively destroyed the Natchez as an independent nation, with survivors absorbed into Chickasaw, Creek, and other neighboring peoples.
key_figures: Tattooed Serpent (Natchez war chief, deceased 1725, whose death weakened Franco-Natchez relations)
Sieur de Chépart (French commandant of Fort Rosalie)
Étienne de Périer (Governor of French Louisiana)

sources.name: Antoine-Simon Le Page du Pratz, Histoire de la Louisiane (1758), based on his residence among the Natchez 1718–1734
Letters of Étienne de Périer to the Compagnie des Indes (1729–1730), preserved in French colonial archives
Patricia Galloway, Choctaw Genesis, 1500–1700 (1995) and related ethnohistorical scholarship

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary French accounts and administrative records survive. Natchez oral perspectives are less well documented but corroborated through ethnohistorical research.

events.title: North Carolina becomes a separate Royal Colony
events.description: In 1729, seven of the eight Lords Proprietors of Carolina sold their shares back to the British Crown, finalizing the separation of North Carolina as a distinct royal colony under direct Crown governance. South Carolina had already received a royal governor in 1720 following settler unrest and the Yamasee War, but the northern territory remained nominally proprietary. The purchase price was £17,500 plus arrears. Only Lord Carteret (later Earl Granville) retained his share, which became the Granville District in northern North Carolina. Royal governance brought more systematic land distribution, judicial administration, and integration into the British colonial system, though it also increased Crown-directed taxation and regulation that would generate friction over the following decades.
key_figures: George Burrington (first Royal Governor of North Carolina, appointed 1730)
Earl of Carteret / Lord Granville (retaining proprietor)

sources.name: Colonial Records of North Carolina, ed. William L. Saunders (1886–1890)
Hugh T. Lefler and William S. Powell, Colonial North Carolina: A History (1973)

events.certainty_note: Crown purchase records and proprietary deeds survive. The exact completion date in 1729 is established, though the transfer process extended over several months.
cross_references: 1720-xxx (South Carolina becomes royal colony de facto)

events.title: Bach premieres the St. Matthew Passion in Leipzig
events.region: Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire
events.description: On Good Friday, 15 April 1729, Johann Sebastian Bach's Matthäus-Passion (St. Matthew Passion, BWV 244) received its likely first performance at the Thomaskirche in Leipzig. Scored for double chorus, double orchestra, soloists, and a ripieno choir, it sets the Passion narrative from the Gospel of Matthew with interpolated arias and chorales. The work is now regarded as one of the supreme achievements of Western sacred music, though it received little attention after Bach's death until Felix Mendelssohn's famous 1829 revival. Bach also assumed direction of the Collegium Musicum in Leipzig in 1729, a student ensemble originally founded by Telemann, which became his primary vehicle for secular concert performances.

sources.name: Autograph score of the St. Matthew Passion (BWV 244), held in the Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin
Christoph Wolff, Johann Sebastian Bach: The Learned Musician (2000)
Leipzig church records and concert announcements (Thomaskirche archives)

events.certainty_note: The 1729 Good Friday date is the scholarly consensus for the premiere, but no surviving program or newspaper notice directly confirms the exact first performance date. An earlier version may have been performed in 1727. The revised version in the extant autograph score dates from the early 1730s.

events.title: Jonathan Swift publishes A Modest Proposal
events.description: In 1729, Jonathan Swift published A Modest Proposal for Preventing the Children of Poor People from Being a Burthen to Their Parents or Country, a devastating satirical pamphlet suggesting that impoverished Irish families sell their children as food to wealthy English landlords. The work attacked English exploitation of Ireland, the callous political arithmetic of mercantilist policymakers, and the indifference of the Anglo-Irish ruling class to widespread famine and poverty. It became the most famous work of sustained irony in the English language and remains a foundational text in the study of political satire. The pamphlet reflected genuine crisis: Ireland's economy was severely depressed by English trade restrictions, and rural poverty was acute.
key_figures: Jonathan Swift (Dean of St. Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin; author)

sources.name: Jonathan Swift, A Modest Proposal (Dublin, 1729), first edition pamphlet
Claude Rawson, God, Gulliver, and Genocide: Barbarism and the European Imagination, 1492–1945 (2001)

events.certainty_note: First edition survives; publication date established by Dublin bookseller records and contemporary references.

events.title: Founding of Baltimore Town in Maryland
events.description: On 8 August 1729, the Maryland colonial assembly authorized the establishment of Baltimore Town on the Patapsco River, naming it after the Calvert family's title, Baron Baltimore. The initial plat comprised 60 acres purchased from Charles and Daniel Carroll. The town was created as a port for the tobacco trade and grain export, taking advantage of the deep-water harbor at the head of the Chesapeake Bay. Growth was slow in the first decades, but by mid-century Baltimore had become one of the fastest-growing towns in the colonies, driven by flour milling and Atlantic trade. It would eventually become one of the largest cities in the United States.
key_figures: Charles Calvert, 5th Baron Baltimore (Lord Proprietor of Maryland)
Charles Carroll (landowner who sold the original acreage)

sources.name: Acts of the Maryland General Assembly, 1729 (Maryland State Archives)
J. Thomas Scharf, History of Baltimore City and County (1881)

events.certainty_note: Legislative act survives in the Maryland State Archives. Date well attested.

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor intensifies anti-Christian measures in China
events.description: By 1729, the Yongzheng Emperor had significantly escalated the expulsion of Christian missionaries from China's interior provinces, continuing policies initiated with his 1724 edict banning Christianity. Missionaries were confined to Macau or to the small group retained in Beijing for their scientific and cartographic expertise. Provincial officials conducted searches for hidden churches and catechists. The crackdown reflected both the Chinese Rites controversy (the papal rejection of Confucian-compatible Catholic practice) and Yongzheng's broader suspicion of heterodox religious movements, including millenarian Buddhist and sectarian societies that he saw as threats to social order. The suppression interrupted over a century of Jesuit cultural exchange and set the pattern for Qing religious policy through the 18th century.
key_figures: Various Jesuit missionaries confined to Beijing (e.g., Ignatius Kögler, court astronomer)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty), Yongzheng reign section
David E. Mungello, The Great Encounter of China and the West, 1500–1800 (2009)
Jesuit letters and reports from the China mission, 1720s–1730s (Lettres édifiantes et curieuses)

events.certainty_note: Qing administrative records and Jesuit correspondence both document the intensification of anti-Christian measures during this period. Specific provincial enforcement varied.
cross_references: 1742-xxx (Pope Benedict XIV's Ex quo singulari ends Chinese Rites accommodation)

events.title: Bajirao I and the Maratha expansion toward Malwa and Gujarat
events.description: Throughout 1729, Peshwa Bajirao I continued the aggressive Maratha military campaigns that were dismembering the Mughal provinces of central and western India. Maratha forces consolidated control over Malwa and raided deep into Gujarat, extracting chauth (tribute) and establishing administrative footholds. The Mughal governor of the Deccan, Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I, was caught between resisting Maratha encroachment and managing his own semi-autonomous position vis-à-vis the weak Mughal court in Delhi. Bajirao's campaigns in this period laid the groundwork for the decisive Battle of Palkhed (1728, continuing maneuvering into 1729) which had forced the Nizam to cede Maratha rights to revenue collection across vast territories.
key_figures: Bajirao I (Peshwa of the Maratha Empire)
Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I (Mughal governor of the Deccan, de facto independent ruler of Hyderabad)

sources.name: Marathi bakhar (chronicle) literature, including accounts of the Peshwa's campaigns
G. S. Sardesai, New History of the Marathas, Vol. II (1948)
Mughal administrative correspondence (akhbarat) from the Delhi court

events.certainty_note: Both Maratha and Mughal administrative records attest to the campaigns. Specific battle dates within the year are less precisely documented than the overall trajectory.
cross_references: 1737-xxx (Bajirao's raid reaches the walls of Delhi)

events.title: Corsican revolt against Genoese rule begins
events.description: In late 1729, widespread popular unrest erupted in Corsica against the Republic of Genoa's heavy taxation and neglectful governance. The revolt was sparked in the interior village of Bozio when an elderly man reportedly refused a tax collector's demand, triggering broader resistance among village communities. The insurrection spread rapidly through the mountainous interior, where Genoese authority had always been thin. This uprising inaugurated the Corsican struggle for independence that would persist intermittently for four decades, producing the short-lived Corsican Republic (1755–1769) under Pasquale Paoli, whose constitutional experiment would later influence the American Founders.

sources.name: Peter Thrasher, Pasquale Paoli: An Enlightened Hero, 1725–1807 (1970)
Genoese administrative correspondence regarding Corsica (Archivio di Stato di Genova)

events.certainty_note: Genoese records and later Corsican sources agree on the timing and initial causes. The precise triggering incident has some legendary embellishment in later accounts.

events.title: Ottoman Tulip Era continues under Ahmed III
events.description: In 1729, the Ottoman Empire was in the final phase of the 'Tulip Era' (Lale Devri), a period of relative peace, cultural florescence, and Western-influenced reform under Sultan Ahmed III and Grand Vizier Nevşehirli Damat İbrahim Pasha. The same year saw the establishment of the first Ottoman printing press by İbrahim Müteferrika, a Hungarian-born convert to Islam, who received permission to print books in Ottoman Turkish (though not religious texts). His first printed book appeared in 1729. The printing press was a significant technological transfer, though its impact was limited by conservative opposition and the exclusion of religious works. The Tulip Era would end abruptly with the Patrona Halil Revolt in September 1730.

sources.name: İbrahim Müteferrika, printed works and his memorandum Usûlü'l-Hikem fî Nizâmi'l-Ümem (Rational Bases for the Politics of Nations)
Şemdanizade Fındıklılı Süleyman Efendi, Müri't-Tevarih (Ottoman chronicle)
Orlin Sabev (Orhan Salih), İbrahim Müteferrika ya da ilk Osmanlı Matbaa Serüveni (2006)

events.certainty_note: Müteferrika's first printed book (Vankulu Lugati, a dictionary) appeared in January–February 1729. The printing privilege is documented in an imperial firman.
cross_references: 1730-xxx (Patrona Halil Revolt ends the Tulip Era)

events.title: Dahoméy kingdom consolidates power in West Africa
events.description: By the late 1720s, King Agaja of Dahomey had consolidated his conquests of the coastal Aja kingdoms of Allada (taken 1724) and Whydah (taken 1727), establishing Dahomey as the dominant power in the Slave Coast region. In 1729, Agaja faced continued resistance from displaced Whydah royalists and raids from the Oyo Empire, which regarded the coastal conquests as threatening its own tributary relationships. European slave traders at the coast had to negotiate new terms with the Dahomean state. Agaja's exact motivations remain debated: some scholars argue he sought to control and potentially limit the slave trade; others contend he aimed to monopolize it. Regardless, Dahomey's rise fundamentally restructured the political economy of the Slave Coast for the remainder of the 18th century.
key_figures: Huffon (deposed King of Whydah, in exile)

sources.name: European trading-post correspondence (Dutch, English, and French factory records from Whydah/Ouidah)
Robin Law, The Slave Coast of West Africa 1550–1750 (1991)
Edna G. Bay, Wives of the Leopard: Gender, Politics, and Culture in the Kingdom of Dahomey (1998)

events.certainty_note: European factory records provide contemporary commercial and political reporting. Dahomean oral traditions supplement but were recorded later. Exact chronology of Agaja's campaigns within individual years is sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Peter II rules Russia; Dolgorukov faction dominates court
events.description: In 1729, the young Tsar Peter II (aged 14) was under the overwhelming influence of the Dolgorukov family, who had displaced Alexander Menshikov from power in 1727. The court had moved back to Moscow from St. Petersburg, symbolically retreating from Peter the Great's Westernizing reforms. Ivan Dolgorukov, the Tsar's close companion, and his father Alexei Dolgorukov maneuvered to have the Tsar betrothed to Ekaterina Dolgorukova. Political governance was largely neglected as the young tsar pursued hunting and festivities. This period of aristocratic reaction would end abruptly with Peter II's death from smallpox on 30 January 1730, leading to the accession of Anna Ioannovna and the brief attempt to impose constitutional conditions on the monarchy.

sources.name: Diplomatic dispatches from foreign ambassadors in Moscow (British, French, and Austrian), 1729
Lindsey Hughes, Russia in the Age of Peter the Great (1998)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in foreign diplomatic correspondence and Russian court records. The move to Moscow and Dolgorukov dominance are well attested.
cross_references: 1730-xxx (Death of Peter II, accession of Anna)

events.title: Nader Qoli consolidates power in Persia under Tahmasp II
events.description: Through 1729, the military commander Nader Qoli (later Nader Shah) continued his campaign to reunify Safavid Persia under the nominal authority of Shah Tahmasp II. In November 1729, Nader won a decisive victory over the Afghan Ghilzai occupiers at the Battle of Damghan, followed by further victories at Murchakhort, driving the Afghans out of Isfahan and most of central Persia. These campaigns effectively ended the Hotaki Afghan occupation that had toppled the Safavid dynasty in 1722. Nader emerged as the real power behind Tahmasp II's restored throne, setting the stage for his own eventual assumption of the crown in 1736 and the creation of the Afsharid Empire.
key_figures: Nader Qoli / Tahmasp Qoli Khan (military commander, future Nader Shah)
Ashraf Hotaki (Afghan ruler of Isfahan, defeated)

sources.name: Mirza Mahdi Khan Astarabadi, Tarikh-e Jahangushay-e Naderi (History of Nader the World-Conqueror), c. 1740s
Michael Axworthy, The Sword of Persia: Nader Shah, from Tribal Warrior to Conquering Tyrant (2006)
Dutch East India Company (VOC) correspondence from Isfahan trading post

events.certainty_note: The battles of Damghan and Murchakhort are dated by multiple sources including European trading-company records. The chronicle by Astarabadi was commissioned by Nader himself and shows some bias but is corroborated by independent sources.
cross_references: 1722-xxx (Afghan conquest of Isfahan ends Safavid rule)

events.title: Montevideo formally founded by Bruno Mauricio de Zabala
events.region: Río de la Plata / Spanish South America
events.description: In late 1729 (with the formal process extending into 1730), the Spanish governor of Buenos Aires, Bruno Mauricio de Zabala, completed the establishment of Montevideo as a permanent settlement on the northern shore of the Río de la Plata. The founding was motivated by the need to counter Portuguese expansion from Colónia do Sacramento (established 1680) and to secure Spanish control of the estuary. Families were brought from Buenos Aires and the Canary Islands to populate the settlement. Montevideo would grow into the principal port of the Banda Oriental (present-day Uruguay) and eventually become the capital of an independent nation.
key_figures: Bruno Mauricio de Zabala (Governor of Buenos Aires)

sources.name: Zabala's correspondence with the Viceroy of Peru and colonial administrative records (Archivo General de Indias, Seville)
Juan E. Pivel Devoto, Historia de la República Oriental del Uruguay (1945)

events.certainty_note: The founding process of Montevideo spanned 1724–1730 in stages (initial military outpost, civilian settlement, formal city establishment). The commonly cited dates of 1724 and 1726 refer to earlier phases; 1729–1730 marks the consolidation with civilian families. Exact dating depends on which phase is considered the 'founding.'

events.title: Siam under King Thai Sa: trade and Buddhist patronage
events.description: In 1729, Siam under King Thai Sa (Borommakot's predecessor on the Ayutthaya throne) maintained active maritime trade with China, the VOC (Dutch East India Company), and regional partners through the royal port of Ayutthaya. Thai Sa's reign was characterized by Buddhist piety, substantial temple construction, and a relatively stable domestic order, though succession anxieties were building. European sources note continued Siamese exports of tin, sappanwood, deer hides, and rice. The kingdom's trade networks linked it to Qing China, Tokugawa Japan (through intermediaries), and the Malay-archipelago sultanates, making Ayutthaya one of the major commercial hubs of mainland Southeast Asia.

sources.name: VOC factory records and correspondence from Ayutthaya (Dutch East India Company archives, Nationaal Archief, The Hague)
Royal Autograph Annals of Ayutthaya (Phraratchaphongsawadan)
Chris Baker and Pasuk Phongpaichit, A History of Ayutthaya (2017)

events.certainty_note: VOC records provide commercial detail but limited political coverage. Ayutthaya court chronicles were compiled later and sometimes lack precision for this period. No major singular event is attested for 1729 specifically.

events.title: Tokugawa Yoshimune's Kyōhō Reforms continue in Japan
events.description: In 1729, Shōgun Tokugawa Yoshimune continued his Kyōhō Reforms (1716–1736), a comprehensive program of fiscal austerity, legal codification, and agricultural improvement aimed at stabilizing the bakufu's finances after decades of deficit spending. In this year, Yoshimune relaxed the longstanding ban on the import of Western (particularly Dutch) books, provided they did not concern Christianity. This modest but consequential liberalization allowed Japanese scholars to access European works on medicine, astronomy, botany, and military technology, seeding the 'Dutch Studies' (Rangaku) movement that would flower later in the century. He also continued efforts to increase rice production and stabilize rice prices, earning the nickname 'the Rice Shōgun.'

sources.name: Tokugawa Jikki (True Records of the Tokugawa), official bakufu chronicle
Timon Screech, Secret Memoirs of the Shoguns: Isaac Titsingh and Japan, 1779–1822 (2006)
Donald Keene, The Japanese Discovery of Europe, 1720–1830 (1969)

events.certainty_note: The relaxation of the Western book ban is traditionally dated to 1720 with the initial decree, but its practical implementation expanded through the 1720s. The broader Kyōhō Reforms are well documented in bakufu records. Attribution of specific measures to 1729 specifically is approximate within the reform program.
cross_references: 1720-xxx (initial relaxation of Western book import ban)

events.title: Ethiopian Emperor Bakaffa's reign and Gondarine culture
events.description: In 1729, Emperor Bakaffa ruled the Ethiopian Empire from his capital at Gondar, presiding over a period of relative stability and cultural refinement in the Gondarine era. Bakaffa was notable for his effective governance and for his marriage to Empress Mentewab, who would become one of the most powerful women in Ethiopian political history after his death in 1730. The Gondarine period was marked by distinctive architecture (the stone castles of Gondar), a flourishing of Ethiopian manuscript art, and continued theological debates within the Ethiopian Orthodox Church. However, Bakaffa also faced challenges from regional lords whose growing autonomy foreshadowed the Zemene Mesafint (Era of the Princes) that would weaken imperial authority from the mid-18th century onward.

sources.name: Royal chronicles of Gondar (Ethiopian court chronicles, compiled by church scholars)
James Bruce, Travels to Discover the Source of the Nile (1790), based partly on Ethiopian royal chronicles
Donald Crummey, Land and Society in the Christian Kingdom of Ethiopia (2000)

events.certainty_note: Ethiopian royal chronicles provide a narrative framework, but their chronology can be imprecise and they are court-centered documents. Bakaffa's reign dates are established; specific events within 1729 are less precisely dateable.
cross_references: 1730-xxx (Death of Bakaffa, beginning of Mentewab's regency)

events.title: Benjamin Franklin acquires the Pennsylvania Gazette
events.description: On 2 October 1729, Benjamin Franklin and his partner Hugh Meredith purchased the failing Universal Instructor in All Arts and Sciences and Pennsylvania Gazette from Samuel Keimer, quickly renaming it simply the Pennsylvania Gazette. Under Franklin's editorship, the paper became one of the most successful and influential newspapers in colonial America, known for its wit, public-interest reporting, and commercial advertising. Franklin used the Gazette as a platform for civic projects, political commentary, and the promotion of his printing business. The acquisition marked a key step in Franklin's rise as a public figure and established a model for newspaper journalism in the American colonies.

sources.name: Benjamin Franklin, Autobiography (written 1771–1790, published posthumously 1791)
Pennsylvania Gazette issues from October 1729 onward (surviving copies in Library Company of Philadelphia and other archives)
Walter Isaacson, Benjamin Franklin: An American Life (2003)

events.certainty_note: Franklin's own account in the Autobiography, corroborated by surviving newspaper issues and business records.

events.title: Stono-region slave importation peaks in South Carolina
events.description: The late 1720s saw a dramatic surge in the importation of enslaved Africans into South Carolina, with the colony receiving thousands of captives annually through Charleston. By 1729, enslaved people outnumbered free white colonists in the colony by roughly two to one, a demographic ratio that generated persistent anxiety among the planter class. Many of the enslaved were brought from the Kongo-Angola region and the Rice Coast (present-day Sierra Leone, Guinea), carrying agricultural knowledge that proved essential to the rice and indigo plantation economy. This demographic imbalance and the cultural cohesion of African-born populations set the conditions for the Stono Rebellion a decade later, the largest slave uprising in British North America before the Revolution.

sources.name: Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database (slavevoyages.org), voyage records for Charleston, 1725–1735
Peter H. Wood, Black Majority: Negroes in Colonial South Carolina from 1670 through the Stono Rebellion (1974)
South Carolina colonial records, including governor's reports to the Board of Trade

events.certainty_note: Ship manifests, colonial census estimates, and governor's correspondence establish the demographic trend. Exact annual figures have some uncertainty due to incomplete records and smuggling.

events.title: Stephen Gray discovers electrical conduction
events.description: In 1729, the English dyer and amateur scientist Stephen Gray discovered the principle of electrical conduction — that the 'electric virtue' (static charge) could be transmitted along certain materials (conductors) but not others (insulators). Working at the Charterhouse in London, Gray demonstrated that electrical charge generated by rubbing a glass tube could be transmitted along packthread, wire, and the human body over distances exceeding 200 feet. His experiments, communicated to the Royal Society, represented a fundamental advance in understanding electricity, distinguishing for the first time between conductors and insulators. Gray's work laid essential groundwork for the experiments of Dufay, Nollet, and ultimately Benjamin Franklin.
key_figures: Granville Wheler (collaborator, Fellow of the Royal Society)

sources.name: Stephen Gray, 'A Letter to Cromwell Mortimer, M.D., Secr. R.S., containing several Experiments concerning Electricity,' Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, Vol. 37 (1731–1732), reporting experiments from 1729–1730
J. L. Heilbron, Electricity in the 17th and 18th Centuries: A Study of Early Modern Physics (1979)

events.certainty_note: Gray's own letters and published papers in the Philosophical Transactions document his experiments in detail. The key experiments are firmly dated to 1729.

disconfirming_evidence: The premiere date of Bach's St. Matthew Passion (1729-004) is contested: some musicologists have argued for a 1727 first performance of an earlier version, making the 1729 performance a revision rather than a true premiere. The debate hinges on the interpretation of a surviving performing-parts watermark analysis by Alfred Dürr and subsequent scholarship. The significance attributed to İbrahim Müteferrika's printing press (1729-010) has been nuanced by scholars who note that the press produced only 17 titles over its first two decades and had negligible impact on Ottoman literacy rates; framing it as a transformative moment overstates its immediate effect. Regarding the Natchez Revolt (1729-002), Le Page du Pratz's account — the most detailed narrative source — was written decades later and includes embellishments and possible fabrication of dialogue; modern ethnohistorians treat it cautiously. Agaja of Dahomey's motivations (1729-011) are genuinely disputed: the thesis that he sought to limit the slave trade (advanced by I.A. Akinjogbin in 1967) has been substantially challenged by Robin Law and others who argue that Agaja's conquests aimed at monopolizing, not restricting, slave exports. The Montevideo founding date (1729-014) is debated among Uruguayan historians, with rival claims for 1724, 1726, and 1730 depending on which phase of settlement is privileged.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1729 CE is rich for Europe, the Ottoman Empire, and European colonial zones, where state archives, diplomatic correspondence, trading-company records, and early newspapers survive in abundance. For East Asia, Qing administrative records (Veritable Records, Grand Council archives) and Tokugawa bakufu records provide substantial documentation, though they are court-centered and tend to obscure popular experience. South Asian coverage relies heavily on Maratha-era bakhars and Mughal akhbarat, which are partisan and administratively focused. Sub-Saharan African history for this period depends disproportionately on European trading-post records, which filter African events through commercial interests and racial prejudice. Indigenous American perspectives on events like the Natchez Revolt are reconstructed primarily through ethnohistorical methods, as the Natchez did not have a written tradition. The surviving sources for 1729 are overwhelmingly elite, male, and state-centered; the lives of women, peasants, the enslaved, and non-literate peoples are visible mainly through demographic inference and material culture.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Seville restored commercial privileges originally granted at Utrecht in 1713
Unresolved tensions over smuggling and Gibraltar led to the War of Jenkins' Ear in 1739
French retaliatory campaign in 1730 destroyed the Natchez as an independent nation
Mendelssohn's 1829 revival brought the St. Matthew Passion to wide recognition
Bajirao's 1729 campaigns followed from his decisive victory at Palkhed in 1728
1729 revolt began the Corsican independence struggle leading to Paoli's republic in 1755
The Tulip Era's cultural spending contributed to the popular backlash of the 1730 Patrona Halil Revolt
Nader's campaigns were a direct response to the Afghan Hotaki conquest of Isfahan in 1722
Military victories in 1729 set the stage for Nader's seizure of the crown in 1736
The demographic imbalance and African cultural cohesion set conditions for the 1739 Stono Rebellion
Gray's discovery of conduction was foundational to Franklin's later electrical experiments
Peter II's death in January 1730 ended the Dolgorukov ascendancy and triggered a succession crisis
Franklin's growing influence through the Gazette led to founding the Library Company in 1731

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
